questions.text: I sense when a group has collectively decided on something before any formal vote or discussion.
I do not naturally sense when it is my turn to speak versus when I should hold back.
I find that my emotional reactions match the scale of the event rather than exceeding it.
I feel that being willing to abandon a failing approach is more valuable than consistency.
I believe that the moral priority of suffering is the principle I would invoke first when trying to explain my values to someone who does not share them.
I routinely decline social events that don't advance my personal interests.
I do not pick up on unspoken hierarchies in social groups.
I routinely choose personal development over group activities.
I like to take time and let the group deliberate before a decision is made.
I view deferring to others as a service to the group.
I believe that the capacity to process suffering intellectually, without emotional involvement, is what makes sustained professional service possible.
I believe that my ability to reason clearly about human problems depends on not being drawn into the emotional current of those problems.
I hold the view that my life's meaning is directly proportional to my contribution to others.
I feel that a predictable, well-ordered life is a peaceful life.
I think of authority as something to be exercised, not just held.
I do my best work under time pressure with minimal preparation.
I experience life at a comfortable volume — engaged but not flooded.
When something wonderful happens, my elation is so strong it can feel almost manic.
